By the end of this training today, you're going to have a whole new set of ideas of the type of products that you can create, how to create those products, how to use those products, and really all the tools and resources necessary to do it. I'm going to cover everything from A to Z in terms of product formats. I'm going to cover all different types of products from written, audio, video, compilation, continuity products. I'm going to talk to you about delivering those products, the layout of the product, branding of the product. I'm going to talk to you about how you actually assemble the product. This is an A to Z training for you. Sit back, get ready to take some notes, and get ready to learn. Let me officially welcome everybody that just got on. Welcome. This is Engineering Profitable Products for Powerful Marketing Funnels. Of course, this is Todd Brown. We're going to talk about product creation from A to Z. The very first thing that I want you to understand is that the reason why I decided to do this is because over the last handful of days, one of the most common questions or one of the most common concerns that I've gotten from marketers is, well, how do I create products or how do I come up with ideas for products? How do I come up with ideas for products for my add-on offers, for my reverse add-on, for my mid-tier add-on, for my thank you page gift offer? How do I come up with these ideas that are going to be profitable in my marketplace? A lot of marketers allow that question to hold them back from reaping the rewards of setting up and having their own marketing funnel. The first thing that I want you to understand that I really want you to grasp is that product creation, as you're going to learn today because I'm going to talk to you about how to have products created for you so you're not creating a word or a video or an audio or anything later on. But product creation today is the easiest part. It's the easiest part of the whole process because there are so many different fast and easy options for creating products today. In fact, today, as I'm going to share with you later on and share with you some resources and tools that you can use, there are more product creators. More people available today willing to give you content that you can use than ever before at extremely cheap prices. The reason is that because the real skill is the marketing, the real skill, the money skill, where the money comes from is the marketing funnel. There are tons of people with great ideas, great concepts that are great educators, but they don't know how to monetize their knowledge. You can very easily tap into their knowledge if you felt like you didn't have the expertise for a particular niche if you wanted to. Don't ever allow the product creation to hold you back because it's truly the easiest part of the entire process. Like I said, I'm going to show you how you can have everything created for you if you truly don't want to create a single piece of content. Very quickly, there are three big product creation mistakes that you need to be aware of upfront. I see these mistakes made all the time. These mistakes that are at the foundation of why so many products fail, so many products basically sit on the shelves, so to speak, and collect dust. The first one is creating products that you want. You come up with an idea and you create a product based on something that you want to teach. You're interested in a topic. You're interested in an area. You're really diving deep into books or trainings or whatever on a particular area. You decide that you want to teach that area, that topic, to your marketplace. You've got to recognize that it's not about creating products you want. When we're talking about creating profitable products, high demand products, products that sell, products that generate income and profit for you, it's not about what you want. It's about knowing what the market wants. I'm going to show you some research tools that you can use in just a bit to uncover ideas for what your market wants and what your market is willing to pay for and what your market is paying for. You've got to recognize that you are not the prospect. That's a writer downer. I know that that might seem like common sense to many of you, but you are not the prospect. You've got to recognize that when it's time to create products, you cannot create the products that you want. You have to create the products that your market wants. I can tell you I've done this early on. I've been online for over 12 years now involved in direct response marketing. I'm going to show you a bunch of successful examples today from my own history and my own companies. I can show you example after example of products that I created early on that were based on what I wanted to create or mistake number two, what I thought the market wanted. I put so much time, energy, and effort into creating the product, creating the market based on what I wanted to teach, based on what I thought the market wanted to learn or wanted to know or wanted from me. Those products didn't do well. In fact, in many cases, those products were bombs. Not only can you not create the products, it's not about you. It's not about you. It's about the market. The second mistake, like I said, is assuming that you know what the market wants. Once you recognize the first mistake and you recognize that, okay, I get it, Todd, it's about creating products that the market wants, well, the second mistake is just assuming that you know, not doing any kind of research or legwork to make sure you understand what it is that the market wants, that you have proof, so to speak, for what the market wants. The third big mistake is really just a mindset thing that you need to get over. That is striving for perfection. What I mean by this is striving for what you think is a product that is finally ready to go to the market. One of the mistakes, and please get this, this is, get this, and I've done this, and so I know. One of the mistakes is looking at what expert marketers are doing, looking at what gurus, so-called gurus are doing, looking at their products, looking at their branding, looking at their platforms that they use to deliver products, and being overwhelmed or intimidated by it, and thinking that your products need to be just like those. Excuse me. Recognize that, and myself included, when starting out, my products didn't look the way they do today. They didn't have the same level of branding, the same look and feel, the same quality of the product. The reality is that I've been able to get to this point as have the majority of experts by putting out enough product and improving as you go. You don't want to look at what the experts are doing and putting out in terms of quality and feel like you have to be up to that standard. You do not. Don't strive for perfection. Don't wait until you think your product is perfect, until it's got every single detail, every single strategy, all of the branding elements that you need. If I were to show you some of the earlier products that I put out, you would be horrified. I look back, and even though there's a level of embarrassment in some of these early companies of the products, I always remind myself that they worked, that they delivered great value to the end user and that they generated money for me while I was delivering value. That's the same mindset that I want you to have. With that being said, let's talk about generating product ideas. I want to give you a few different methods that you can use to generate ideas for your different information products. These are different information products that you can use for your customer captivation offer and all of your different add-on offers as well as your back-end offers. One of the best and most reliable research tools that you have at your disposal are prospect surveys. You should be surveying your prospect list every other month. Every other month, you should be sending out a tiny little two to three-question survey to your prospects so that you can get a better idea of what they want to learn, what they're struggling with, what's preventing them from getting the results that they want. This is a quick aside. One of the things in between those prospect surveys every other month, you should be surveying your customer list. One month, you survey your customer list to find out what, again, they're interested in and every other month, you survey your prospects to find out not only what they're interested in, but their pain points and even concerns and objections that they have about getting started with your main product, let's just say. This is something that when I was running the marketing at strategic profits, we did all the time. We did surveys on a regular basis. We surveyed every new customer coming in to find out as much about them as we possibly can so that we could see trends in similarities between customers. We did tons of prospect surveys looking for ideas for products, looking for concerns and objections and even phraseology that was being used, wording that they were using when talking about why they weren't getting the results that they wanted or why they weren't getting started. I'll tell you, I even used a survey at the beginning of the pre-launch for the Six Figure Funnel Formula Training Program. I think it was three questions just off the top of my head. I asked one of the questions was what stopped you from creating your first funnel? What do you feel you need to learn about setting up funnels? I think I might have asked what could I give you or show you that would make the Six Figure Funnel Formula Training Program the best program, the most valuable program for you? Not only did I get ideas for the actual training itself based on what the market wanted, but I also got and was able to identify specific pain points for the marketing. I got to see that there were a ton of people that were concerned about the time to set up a funnel as well as the expense. That gave me great insight into, well, number one, I knew that the product had to be able to be consumed. The training needs to be able to be consumed in a short period of time as well as to use that particular funnel. It wouldn't require a lot of time and it wouldn't require a lot of money. Prospect surveys are one of the best tools that you can use. Second another tool, and this is in no particular order going forward now. The prospect surveys are the best tool, the most reliable tool for you. The next tool is forums. Any kind of forums in your marketplace, so any kind of discussion groups, forums, Facebook groups, Facebook pages, these all fall under this category. What you want to look for in those groups where your market gathers, you want to look at the common pain points that people are discussing, that people are asking for feedback on, that people are complaining about, or again asking for feedback on. You also want to look for the posts in forums and discussion groups that have the highest rate of comments. What that tells you is that there's a high level of interest with those. It's a topic that those people are interested in, that they're engaged with in your market. They'll begin to give you an idea of what are the common pain points and what are the areas of interest that your market is demonstrating a behavior of interest in. Then there are trade publications and magazines. Some niches, some markets will have trade publications like if you're operating in a professional niche, there are association publications, trade publications, and then there are magazines. Magazines and trade publications are a great resource if they are appropriate for your market for your niche because you can look at a handful of things. Number one, look for the common themes in terms of articles published. If you see that there's a theme, a common theme, or a common trend that continues to show up in these trade publications, in these magazines, that's a good indication that readers are responding to that content. The publishers and editors of those publications know that and that's why they are continuing to put articles and editorial content in there on those topics. The other thing to utilize trade publications and magazines for is to look at the reader feedback where there is reader comments that are usually published in the beginning. Again, if you see common themes in terms of pain points or lots of response as it relates to an article or editorial piece of content, that's a great indication that there is interest there. Lastly, with magazines and trade publications, what you want to look for is you want to look for direct response marketers, not typical institutional advertisers, but you want to look for direct response marketers, marketers that are not trying to brand, but they're trying to generate a response. You want to look for advertisers and direct response marketers that are running ads routinely because direct response marketers are able to do everything by the numbers. They're able to see the return on investment, profit, loss, whatever it may be. Direct response marketers aren't going to run an ad over and over and over if it's not making money. You can leverage what other advertisers are doing by looking at the ads that are continuing to run, looking at the ads, what are those products, and even from a marketing perspective, looking at the themes and appeals and marketing hooks of those different ads that are continuing to run over and over. You can really do just as a quick aside without getting too advanced. You can really do the same thing with PPC advertising, like Google AdWords if you really wanted to by using a tool like SpyFu, for example, spyfu.com, because you can go there and you can look at the advertisers that are advertising for a particular keyword and you can see which advertisers are running ads and which ads they've been running for a long period of time. You know that based on those ads that they're running for a long period of time that they're making money from that. It'll give you insight into the product as well as the marketing. The next research tool is ClickBank. ClickBank is one of the largest online marketplaces for digital information products. You can very easily go to ClickBank and you can see which products are selling the most. You can see which products are sitting on the vine, which ones are collecting dust, and which ones are selling really well. Tells you again the type of product, the topic, and it also gives you insight from a marketing perspective on the hook, the angle that's being used. It gives us insight into how to craft your own hook for that type of product. The next technique is something that I learned from J.Abraham when I was running the marketing for rich at strategic profits. From then on we just called it the J.Abraham Amazon Strategy. The J.Abraham Amazon Strategy is to go over, and this is great. So man, this is great, is to go over to Amazon and look at the different books within your niche that's appropriate for your topic, for your business. The books that are talking about the different topics or topic that you are contemplating or brainstorming on. One of the things that you want to do is, number one, you want to look at the table of contents of those books because it'll give you ideas for your own products as well as the structure of your potential products. But even more valuable is to look at the comments and to look at both the positive comments to see what it is that people liked about the book, about the content, about the structure, and look at the negative comments. Look at the negative comments also for the same, but also to see if there are certain elements that the author left out of the book that people really wanted to see covered. If it was too theoretical, if it was too conceptual, if it was not deep enough, if it was too superficial, if they wanted more exercises or XYZ because again, it gives you ideas for what the market wants and typically from buyers. You can see from people that are actually spending money, what they liked, what they didn't like, what they wanted to see more of, what they felt was missing, great strategy. Then of course, you can utilize Amazon and look at Amazon magazines for ideas within your niche, Amazon books, and the Amazon Kindle store. These are some of the tools that you can use to start to generate product ideas. Let's talk very quickly about, and I just want to touch on this very quickly because this in itself could almost be a full blown training. Developing your products, big idea. One of the things that marketers neglect to do often is they understand, hopefully you understand that it's critical that your marketing funnel be built on a big idea, on a hook, on a marketing angle, a hook that grabs attention, a hook that captivates your audience, your prospects interest. You also need to make sure that your product is being built on a big idea. Typically what we do is we take the hook, the big idea from your marketing funnel and we use that as the product's hook or what sometimes, and this really isn't the same, but it's sometimes referred to as the unique selling proposition of your product. Really what I mean by this is it's not just the hook behind the content of the product because really that's no different from or shouldn't be any different from the hook that you used in your marketing funnel. I'm also talking about how are you positioning the product? Are you positioning the product as an e-book? Are you positioning it as a digital manual? Are you positioning it as a fast start guide? Are you positioning it as a quick start instructional guide? Are you, how are you positioning the product itself? Because you can have a PDF, right? Are you presenting it as a PDF? Are you presenting it as a special report? Are you presenting it as a white paper? All of those things have a different impact on your audience. One of the things that I teach clients, especially my higher level clients, is that how you position your products has an impact on the perceived value. There's a variety of different ways. This is all marketing. There's a variety of different ways to position or rename anything and everything. For example, you could maybe be doing, let's say, a Q&A call. For example, you could call them Q&A calls. You could also call them live coaching calls. You could call them group coaching calls. You could call them group fast start calls. You could call them, there's a variety of different things. You want to make sure that you're thinking through how you're positioning your product. You could call it a transcript. You could call it a manual. You could call it a variety of different things that you can call it. You want to make sure that you are positioning it so that it separates and positioning your different elements so that it separates your product from the pack, from what everybody else is offering out there. Names are critical. Names are critical. What you name your product itself is critical. What you name the different elements. Is it a Q&A call or is it a group coaching call? Is it a transcript or is it a training manual? Is it handouts or is it training exercises? Names are critical. Last thing that I'm going to mention very quickly is that the thing that I really want you to try to focus on to the best of your ability is evergreen products. Products that will not go out of fashion or go out of date quickly. You want evergreen products. You want as close to evergreen products as you possibly can because those are products that you can leverage out the wazoo and sell for long periods of time versus products that are not evergreen or fly by night and might only be viable or might only be sellable for a short period of time. For example, if you created a product on Facebook advertising, the Facebook advertising platform and how to use Facebook advertising, well, clients like my friend Charles Kirkland, he would tell you that because the platform is updated so often, products very quickly go out of date and they're no longer applicable for right now, which means that he either has to stop selling it or he has to update it constantly. You want to look for the evergreen arenas, the evergreen topics within your niche and really focus on those to the best of your ability because those are the type of products that you can sell for years, literally for years. Now, I want to quickly talk about a simple product structure that you can use, whether you're creating written products, video products, audio products, doesn't matter. There are a variety of different structures that you can use for delivering the content. I'm going to give you what I believe is the easiest one. This is really the structure that I used when I was building and creating the Six Figure Funnel Formula Training Program, for example. The structure that I'm about to show you, it's four steps, that's it. This is the type of structure that you use overall for your product and I'll explain what I mean by that in just a second, as well as by section. In other words, as by lesson or by module. The product structure, the easiest product structure is to start off by telling what, meaning what is it that you're going to be talking about? What is it that like in the, for example, let's say you were selling Facebook advertising or something like that. The beginning section is all about what? What you're about to learn is Facebook advertising. You talk about why? Why it's so important that you learn this methodology, this approach, this process that I'm about to teach you. Then you get into the how to, how to actually do it, and then you get into the what to do now. You can have an entire, let's take the easiest example, an entire book for a second and that book is all about this is what you do, it's why you do it, it's how you do it, and then it's what to do now. Then in each chapter, you could have the same thing. Let's say we had a book on writing your own book. The whole book would be about what you're going to do, why you're going to do it, how to do it, and then what to do now. Then in each chapter, the first chapter might be on outlining. You start off by talking about what you're going to do, outline, why you're going to outline, how you're going to outline, and then what to do now. Then in the next chapter, if it was, if the next chapter in the book is about starting, writing your chapters, it's what you're going to do. You're going to begin writing your chapters. Why are you doing it this way? How to actually do it, so how to is the biggest chunk of the content. Then what to do now, and the same for the next chapter, the same for the next lesson. Doesn't matter whether you're using written content, whether you're using video content, audio content, webinar, tele-seminar does not matter. This is one of the easiest and simplest structures that you can use. With that in mind, let's jump into the different types of products that you can create. Let's start by talking about the different types of written products that you can use. There are a variety of different written products. Of course, we've got digital books and physical books. Let me say this, that this particular slide, and there's four slides like this throughout the rest of the training. On slide eight, there are 52 slides in this training just to give you a heads up. These type of slides are to give you an idea or to stimulate ideas for the different types of products for each of these categories that you can create. We've got digital and physical products, a digital book. A digital book is really nothing more than a PDF. On the Mac, you can export any Word document or pages document as a PDF. I'm sure there's an application for PC users. There's also free online PDF converters so that you can take a Word document and convert it into PDF. You never want to deliver an e-book or digital book or anything like that as a Word doc or pages doc because then it's editable. They can edit it. They can change it. They can do a whole bunch of stuff whereas with a PDF, it's locked and so they can. Then we've got short reports. These are reports that are anywhere from seven to 15 pages typically in the 10 to 12 range that are on a very specific narrow topic. The thing with short reports is that you don't want to go wide. You want to go deep and narrow. That's for a lot of reasons. Because number one, it's a short report for a reason. But also because short reports are typically priced on the lower end. You can price short reports or little reports really up to $49 but you'll find that the best results that you get are usually up to $19. At that price point, the psychology of the prospects is that if it's a short report and you are covering a big broad topic, the mindset is how deep can you really go? It's probably superficial content. You want your short reports to be designed around one particular narrow topic where you solve one particular specific problem. You've got short reports. Then you've got email and PDF courses. This is typically viewed as old school where most marketers think that delivering an email course where they get an email once a week for 10 weeks or 8 weeks or 6 weeks is outdated, is outdated or delivering a PDF once a week for 4 weeks or 6 weeks or 8 weeks is dated. I can tell you that one of the most popular email marketing, email copywriting courses on the net from my friend Andre Schaperam is delivered like this. People rave about that training and he sold it like crazy. Don't think that you've got to have a full blown membership site or this is one of the easiest ways to really get started is to have a training that gets delivered once a week via either email or PDF. Then of course there are transcripts where you can take any audio and have it transcribed. You can take any video, have it transcribed. Audios from interviews, expert interviews. You can take audios from videos that you've created. You record a video. If you've got a video. You can extract out the audio. You can just do a Google search for extract audio from video. You can extract out the audio and then have that audio transcribed. Now you've got a written publication. If you've got a video and you want to use the transcript as a bonus, you can do that and you're still able to sell the video separately to other people because at a different price, point because it's a different format and whatnot. A little tip when you are transcribing interviews is don't just provide the transcript. You will have a much greater response from your market when you turn it into more of a book like the book by Napoleon Hill, Think and Grow Rich is really just a series of interviews with some of the industrial heavyweights back then. It reads like a book. It reads like a story like a novel, but it's really just transcripts from conversations. You'll get a heck of a lot more when you are turning your transcripts into these little books. You can also start with private label or resale rights products. This isn't something that I really recommend. This isn't something that I do. There are products. There are product creators like I mentioned earlier on. There are content creators that do not know how to monetize their knowledge. They don't know how to sell it. What they do is they give other people the rights to buy their content and use their content as they want. In some cases, you can take their content and you can repurpose it. You can take their content, edit it, change it, use it as fodder, ideas for your own products, use it as the foundation that you build on. You can go to Google and search for private label rights products and you'll find a ton. Most are crap. Tiny percentage are good. You can also use private label or resale rights products that you edit and tweak a little bit as bonuses, especially just as a side note as unadvertised bonuses. I used to do this all the time in one of my earlier companies where I would go out and find the rights that I could buy to various products on topics that I didn't want to teach or that I didn't know about, but that would still be valuable for my market. I would go out and buy it for them and then I would tell them, hey, I came across this product. I found out that I could buy the rights to this product to be able to give it to you. Here is a little bonus just as my way of over delivering value to you. Unadvertised bonuses are a great stick strategy and leveraging resale rights products and editing them, improving them, not just using them out of the box because like I said, most of them are crap, but repurposing them. Another tip for you is that you can look for other people's products in other niches that might work well in your niche. In other words, if you see that, let's say you are selling to plumbers. I don't know. Let's say you're selling how to market your plumbing business, let's just say. You see that somebody in another niche created a product. How to market your electrician business or how to market your dental business. Well, don't be afraid to approach those product creators, those vendors and ask them if they would be willing to license their product to you so that you could change it and tweak it and use it in your niche. I did that multiple times from one niche to another where we took a product that was appropriate for one niche and when we saw that it was appropriate for another niche, we took it, we repurposed it, we tweaked it slightly and we rolled it out to the other niche. Boom. Dundeeal, no product creation and you've got a full blown product that you can use and roll out to another niche. Then there is public domain. This used to be popular and used to be talked about many moons ago. It's not really talked about much anymore, but a lot of individuals don't realize that there are a tremendous tens of thousands, if not hundreds of thousands of published works that are available that are now in the public domain, meaning that you can use them, do with them what you want. Anything that in the United States was published before 1923 is public domain. I'm going to give you resources where you can go to get this. I remember, if you know Matt Fury, Matt Fury released a product that was a six figure product. He generated well over six figures selling a product on exercise like the farmer burns workout or something like that. Generated well over six figures selling that product, it was found in the public domain. He found it in the public domain, went, took it, republished it because it's public domain. You could do with it what you want and generated a big dough from it. Also products that were published between 1923 and 1963, but their copyright was not renewed, those are in the public domain. There's about 7% of give or take, 7%. That's the latest number that I saw of works published between 1923 and 1963 that never had their copyright renewed that are now in the public domain. Before you use those, you definitely want to ensure that the copyright wasn't renewed. Anything before 1923, like I said, totally public domain. To find public domain books, you can go to eBay Books. If you find a book on eBay that's in public domain, you could use a company like BookScanning.com to take that book and scan it in so that you can then edit it and tweak it. They'll send you a document of that book. You can also use Gutenberg.org to find public domain works. The last thing that I want to talk about as it relates to written products is using voice recognition software, using a voice recognition application. On the Mac, if you're using Mavericks, I guess OS 10.8 or something like that or OS 10, I'm not sure what it is, but Mavericks, it's built in to that operating system. For example, all you got to do is push the function key twice and then talk into a microphone and it'll dictate exactly what you say. This is a side note. This is a great tool for short cutting the info product development process. Many times I'll respond to emails. I'll create the foundation of blog posts, even outlines for products using voice recognition. On the PC, you can use something like Dragon Naturally Speaking. This is an example of a product that I created. I talked about expert interviews and using the transcript. I'm going to talk about this in just a bit. Actually, I might as well just tell you right now. One of the easiest things to do, what I did was years ago, when I first started, like I said, 12 years ago or so, when I started online, I had this company that taught massage therapists how to market their practices. I wanted to create a product on generating referrals. What I did was I went over to Amazon. I looked up the top 10 books on referral generation and I reached out to the authors and I asked them if they'd be willing to be interviewed for our massage therapist on how to generate referrals. Four of the 10 responded yes. I was able to interview them and I took the transcript from each of their interviews and I put it into one product. As corny as it sounds, we called it the Instant Massage Referral Stampede. Keep in mind, this is going back to seven, eight years ago for this particular product. It sold like crazy because not only was it on a topic that the market wanted that I knew from prospect surveys, but I got to leverage the credibility of these top authors and experts. It helped me sell the product. I didn't create a lick of content other than interviewing them. All I did was ask each of them the same nine questions and then we were able to take their answers and compile it into one product. Let's talk about actually what are called compilation products. Compilation products are when you compile a variety of different resources into a single product. For example, you could have a compilation product that's a compilation of articles where those articles, then there's a couple different ways to do this. Check this out. You could have a compilation of your most popular blog posts. If you've been online for any length of time, if you blog on a regular basis, you could take all of your top blog posts and combine them into one digital book. You wouldn't believe how often this is done. I'm going to show you an example in just a minute. I did this. I'll show you A-level copywriter, John Carleton recently did this. You could also use articles that are published on article directory sites like E-Zine articles and there's a ton of them. As long as you are citing the author and you are citing the source, which we're going to talk about, you can collect the best articles out on the web about a particular topic into one manual and sell it. Then there are compilation products like swipe files. This is typically seen in the marketing space where if you assemble a product of all different swipe files of headlines, of subject lines, of whatnot, that's a good product. There are toolkits like ready to use legal forms, ready to use spreadsheets. There are toolkits of pre-done advertisements that are pre-done anything that they can use. There are case studies and stories like Chicken Soup for the Soul. Look at that brand that's built on just a compilation of stories. You could have, if you were selling to gardeners, you could have a compilation of stories or a compilation of case studies from people that grew unbelievable gardens, vegetable gardens, herb gardens, whatever, compilation of templates, a compilation of resources, like a list of resources. Again, go to Barnes & Noble, go to Amazon and you'll see that people buy books on things like directories, for example. There's a site called Directory of Easeens that had a list of every online newsletter that individuals could potentially advertise within or submit a guest article for. People used to pay like crazy, I think it was $90 a year, to get access to this directory of easings. You've got to ask yourself, are there tools? Are there resources? Are there things that your market would love to get access to in the way of a directory? Then there are, of course, cheat sheets, mind maps, flow charts, process maps, even like I said, sticky forum posts. You could go into the top forums, the top groups for your particular market and take the ones that have the highest level of comments, the ones that have the most amount of engagement and you can assemble that into a particular product. The key is that you are always citing the source or the reference when you are doing a compilation product. One of the easiest resources to use is EasyBib.com. EasyBib.com will actually generate the proper reference for you, the proper citation for you. You just copy it, boom, put it into your product. When you're using articles from something like Easeen articles or Article Hub or Article Directory or Article City, you just make sure that you take the author's little bio in the end and you take the web address, the URL of the article. Fast, simple ways to create product. I mentioned John Carlton. Here's an example of his book, right? Selling for $10 on the Kindle, $22 paperback, over 70 reviews, five stars. You could see I highlighted right here. This book is a collection of his best and most recent articles, right? His blog posts is really what it is. People rave about this. The other thing that we're going to come back to later on, which is a little bit of an advanced tactic. I'm going to give you seven advanced tactics is this right here, which we're going to come back to in just a bit. This was an example from the very first information product that I ever created. You could actually see it's got a diskette. That shows you how old this is. Where massage therapist got a marketing toolkit on diskette, that was 42 pre-done, ready to use ads, yellow pages ads, flyers, postcards. This is an example of a compilation product. This is another example from when I used to work with chiropractors, another compilation product. This thing sold like crazy. This was priced at $200 and I think it was $297 or maybe $247. Basically, all this was an email sequence that was five days. It was a five-day email sequence. What they got were they got the five different emails and a video that I recorded, a screen capture video that I recorded breaking down the five different emails. This is an example of a compilation product. I had those emails done that we were using with other clients and I took those emails, I recorded a simple video and boom. This was based on, and this is so important, so please get this. This was based on market research. I knew that at the time, these doctors were, these chiropractors were struggling with patients going inactive. We created a product, look at the positioning of the product. I didn't just call it five emails that you can send out. I called it the five-day reactivation revenue machine. Here's the tagline, the copy and paste email sequence that brings back 56% of your inactive patients in under five days. This was based on the stats, the metrics that we collected from our clients that were using this. Then here's the big promise. Three minutes, send these five proven emails and at least half your inactive patients will call you for appointments, 100% guaranteed. This is the marketing and you can see how it was a compilation product. This thing, like I said, it made us a boatload of money. Now let's talk about creating audio products. There's a variety of different audio products that you can create. You can have products that are audios that are streaming, meaning they can only be listened to online, downloadable. You can have monologue audio products. This is just you speaking on a particular topic. You could have tele-seminars, live tele-seminars, audio recordings of tele-seminars. Do not think for a second that webinars have made tele-seminars outdated because there are lots of products or lots of presentations that are appropriate for webinars because they're very visual. You're going to show images, graphs, charts, lots of proof elements. But then there are tons of presentations that are better delivered via tele-seminar. If there's no need for the graphical elements, you are much better off delivering it via tele-seminar because it is a lot more convenient for prospects. They don't need to be in front of the computer. They can be in their car. They can be sitting on their couch. They can be anywhere. They don't need to be tech savvy to log on to a webinar. All they do is pick up the phone and call. Using a tool like autoteleseminar.com, they can be fully automated. You can run evergreen tele-seminars constantly. There's what I call audio extraction, which I mentioned earlier, where you can extract the audio from a video. If you've got videos that you've recorded for something, you can extract the audio from those videos. Now you can use those audios for something else, for a bonus. We're going to talk about repurposing content later on. Of course, there are interviews, like expert interviews like I did, where you can offer the audio, you can offer the transcript. There are then best practice interviews. Best practices interviews are interviews with, let's say if you're selling to the gardening market, are interviews that you do with other gardeners. How do they till the soil or whatever they do? Or best practice interviews, like if you were again selling to plumbers on how to grow their business, these are interviews that you do with other plumbers, plumbers on your list, plumbers that are customers of yours, about their best practices. Maybe you have five different areas that you talk to five different plumbers about, and that becomes a best practice, best practices interview series. Maybe you ask, how do you get new customers? How do you keep your customers? How do you generate referrals from customers? How do you upsell your customers? Whatever. Maybe those are the only questions that you ask, and you ask five different plumbers, and that becomes a best practices interview series. Then you've got a telesummit, which is more of just a series of teleseminars. It could be a telesummit on a particular topic. My good friend David Frey did this with a variety of different topics like the copywriting telesummit or the fitness telesummit, where maybe one week you've got somebody that's talking about weight training. Another week you've got somebody that's talking about cardio. Another week you've got somebody that's talking about crossfit or functional training. Another week you've got somebody talking about diet. Another week you've got somebody talking about supplementation. Another week you've got somebody talking about rest and recuperation. recuperation. And then you've got what I call the best practices round table, which is instead of a series of separate interviews, this is where you get multiple people on at the same time. So for example, like we've got this bonus coming up for the folks that register for the six figure funnel formula training program, where I'm getting four of the top traffic experts in the world, Jonathan, Micell, Charles Kirkland, Goer, Chaudry and Justin Brooke to come on a live streaming event. And at the same time, go over there, how they would drive tons of traffic into a brand new funnel to turn it very quickly into a six figure funnel. That is an example of a best practices round table. Then there are just one off expert interviews. Now let me give you a couple of resources and tools to do this. I mentioned Amazon, but you want a great way to get top experts to be willing to do an interview with you. Look for the recently published books in your particular topic, in your particular arena and reach out to those authors. Go take that author's name, Google them, go to their site, find their contact me or contact us page and reach out to them. Let them know that you want to interview them for your list of customers, your list of prospects and you think that it will be a great opportunity for them to promote their book. Why does this work so well? Why will you get such a high percentage of these experts to agree to do the interview? Because most of them, when they publish a book, are in this media promotional mindset. They're promoting the book. They're looking to get the word out about their book and so they'll jump at the chance. This was how I've gotten, I can't even tell you how many interviews, how very early on, like eight years ago, how I got Tim Ferriss and tons of top experts on a variety of different time management, referral generation, you name it when you look for the newly published books. You can also look at ClickBank, Contact Folks There. Another great resource from one of my clients, Bill Harrison, is the Radio TV interview report and you can find that at RTIR.com. These are people that are experts in a variety of different categories and areas that are willing to be interviewed because they want to get their name out there. They want to build credibility. They want to really establish themselves. You've got people that will tell you, I'm an expert in this. This is my background. This is my bio and I'd love to do an interview. I could do interviews on these different topics and cover this different material. You can find individuals in there. This makes for great bonuses, great premiums for products and great add-ons. There is the case study type of audio product where you get one of your top students on the phone with you, for example, and I'll tell you the resources that we use to do this in just a bit, and you do a full-blown case study on what did they do, how did they do it, when did they do it, what tools did they use, what was the time frame, what was the thinking behind it, you break it down. This would be the example of if you were again selling to plumbers, if you've got a plumber that rocked the house on your list, a customer, you interview that customer on everything they did, how they did it, when they did it, where they did it, why they did it, and so on. If you don't have a customer list, you can even run an advertisement. I've done this before where I ran an ad in a particular niche. I think it was for carpet cleaners where I was willing to pay them 100 bucks for a one-hour interview over the phone with me. They had to meet certain qualifications in order to get paid, but big deal, I paid them, got their case study, and then that became a paid product. I wasn't using it as a testimonial or as an endorsement. I was getting the content from them on what they did to grow their business, and then I was using that as a product. There's a voiceover, meaning that if you've got a written product, if you've got a book, you can go to something like voiceover or voiceovers.com and you can hire somebody just to do the voiceover for you. You can do an audio book where if you've got your book, you can either record it yourself or again do the voiceover. Of course, there are CDs, and I'll show you a sick resource in just a bit that you can use to fulfill one-off CDs. This was an example of a product that I did, one of the very first products that I did You can see that all I'm saying over here is that five of the top of the world's top massage marketing and practice building experts gathered together to reveal their latest and most effective client attraction secrets. Down here, it got cut off, but down here I talked about who those experts were. You could see is what would you pay to sneak into a closed-door meeting and eavesdrop on? These were experts. This was an expert round table product. I didn't create the content. All I did was moderate this whole experience and ask the questions. This is a series of interviews with guys like Brian Tracy and Jack Lillane and Dennis Waitley and Jack Canfield. This was just a compilation audio product. These were all audio interviews sold also, sold for about 300 and some odd dollars. This was in the very first training program that I ever created, the very first info product I created. This was a bonus that they got. This was a collection of individual interviews. I went to this author of this particular book and I interviewed her. Now, mind you, I had nothing in this niche. I didn't have a single customer at this point when I was creating this product. Don't think that you have to go to these people with, in your particular niche, I don't care what the niche is. Don't think that you've got to go. These people will be, if you approach them and you say, I know that you are respected in this niche, I have an appreciation for what it is that you do. I think that people would get a tremendous amount from hearing from you. I would love to interview and feature you as one of our top experts. People will be flattered if you approach them with that. I was able to get this author. I got the president at the time of the Massage Therapy Association. I went to one of the most popular sites and got the creators of that site. Another author over here, another popular site. Created, did interviews with them and was able to offer it as a bonus. Again, I didn't create any of that content. I simply moderated it. Then of course, as you know, as I already mentioned, then we've got this bad boy coming up for the Six Figure Funnel Formula folks and other experts round table. Let me tell you some of the tools that I use at marketingfunnelautomation.com to create audio products. The microphone, and this is in order of least expensive to most expensive. The microphone that I'm using right now is the Yeti Blue Mic. That's the URL where you can find it. It's probably 120 bucks or something like that. You might even be able to find it on sale. The next mic up from that is the Rode Podcaster. Finally, the Mac Daddy. If you want to sound like you are on a radio station for podcasting and whatnot, is the Hile PR40. That microphone is about $400, so I don't recommend that you start with that. The software that you can use for doing your audio products, you can use Audacity, which is free. You can go right there and download Audacity. In-house, we use Adobe Audition. You really don't need to. We've used it for years and it just has some great noise reduction. If you're in a room with an air condition or a fan going or there's background humming or something like that. Adobe Audition is able to remove that noise really well. Audacity can as well, just not as good as Adobe Audition. In terms of doing interviews and recording those interviews, we use Instant Tele-Seminar, InstantTele-Seminar.com. Great application. That's the application we use to do all of our interviews and record them, as well as to run all of our Q&A calls, all of our coaching calls, all powered through Instant Tele-Seminar. Great little app. People can call in live. People can also listen to it live online and submit questions online. It's actually very cool. Now, let's talk about creating video products. Of course, similar to audio products, you've got streaming video where they can watch it online as well as downloadable. Then there are keynote and PowerPoint videos like what you're watching right now. There are screen capture where they get to see everything that's happening on your desktop. You see this often with software or with certain marketing videos. Then there are webinars. There are multiple webinars. I've created and I'm going to talk to you about this later on. I've done training programs that were, let's say, four webinars, one webinar a week for four weeks. The beauty of doing something like that is that you get paid in advance. You decide that you want to do a webinar training based on what your market wants on a particular topic. You create the marketing for it. You create the video sales letter, the long form sales letter, whatever it is that you're going to use. You sell people on the multiple webinars. You let them know it's live. They'll get access to the live webinars as well as the recordings afterwards. You get paid in advance before you actually create a single piece of content when you do that. Then of course there are the tele-summits. There are webinar summits where you, just like I said with the tele-summits where you have a series of webinars with, it could be with you. It could be with more commonly it's with a bunch of experts that get on and do different presentations week after week after week. It could be four weeks, six weeks, eight weeks. There are live streaming events. These are, I don't recommend that you do these out of the gate because they're a beast and they can be expensive if you're using a streaming service. Very similar to those are today you can do Google Hangouts. For example, tomorrow we have an eight hour tremendous event that we're doing with seven or eight of the top marketers in the online community where they'll be coming on one at a time on a live Google Hangout with me and you'll get access to that. You can actually come on that and hang out with us. It's going to be awesome. We're going to be talking about a variety of different funnel components, email marketing and video creation continuity, the whole nine. Anyway, Google Hangouts are free and so they're not as reliable as something like GoToWebinar which is what we're using right now, but they are very cool and they serve a different purpose. Now, the application that I use, that we use in-house right now to do all of our Google Hangouts is webinar ignition. We're about to start testing my buddy Mike Phil Sames webinar jam, but for right now we use webinar ignition and love it from Mark Thompson. I can't recommend it enough. All of the Google Hangouts that we've done over the last two months I would say have all been powered by webinar ignition. It's just a fantastic plug-in. Then there's Moving Media which is basically like the type of videos that you went through the four videos that I released as part of the pre-launch for the Six Figure Funnel Formula Training Program. There are on-camera videos where you're actually on camera. There are the recordings of live events. If you have a live seminar, like an in-person seminar, boot camp, mastermind, group coaching session, you can have those recorded. There are, and then of course there are DVDs. Now a great resource, an awesome resource for you to use. I can't recommend it enough, is a company called Disc Delivered. Their parent company is Speaker Fulfillment Services which I'm going to mention later on. What's awesome about Disc Delivered, check this out, is this is from my friend Brett Ridgeway and I used Brett's Speaker Fulfillment Services to fulfill all of our physical products. So all of our home study courses that we used to have in all of our different companies, we had fulfilled through Speaker Fulfillment Services. Now Disc Delivered, if you ever wanted to have a DVD or a CD or two CDs or two DVDs that you sell as a $19 product or $29 product and you didn't know where to go to do it, DiscDelivered.com which is Brett's company will fulfill those for you at like, it's for a single DVD or single audio CD, it's $5.99 that includes shipping and handling in the US, $6.99 international and they'll do one off for you. So you don't have to have 500 created or 1,000 created, they'll do one off for you and they'll ship them out for you. So as your orders come in, you don't have to outlay a huge amount of money, I think it's $50 to sign up for DiscDelivered.com, I get no money from this by the way. And they will fulfill, they'll duplicate them, fulfill them for you, all you got to do is pay the $50, you send them the master and there are tons of different ways, you can just Google Create DVD. There are tons of different ways to do that, they'll then fulfill them for you one off for $5.99 or $6.99. So you can charge shipping and handling of just $5.99 or $6.99 if you wanted to and it doesn't cost you anything and you're able to deliver a DVD or CD or you can charge $19.99 and then you make money off of every single DVD or CD that goes out. Now some of the tools that we use for screen capture, we use ScreenFlow, so all of our screen capture when we're doing Keynote or PowerPoint, we use ScreenFlow, if you're on the PC you can use Camtasia. Now the camera that I use when I do the on-camera videos, so if you've ever seen an on-camera video from me where I'm actually on the camera in front of our white border, in front of our flip chart, I use the Panasonic GH2. There are a lot of different cameras that you can use, but this is the camera that I use and love it. The film quality is just off the charts. You can also use your iPhone. So I've done videos on the iPhone. I use the Filmic Pro app. That's the app that I use from the app store. And today, if you're using an iPhone 5, you can get great video and there are apps that you can use even to enhance the video even more. But for this launch of the Six Figure Funnel Formula Training Program, I actually created videos for our partners, for our VIPJV partners using the iPhone and came out phenomenal. Now the mic that I use when we're doing video, like let's say when I'm using the Panasonic GH2, if I'm doing an on-camera video, I use what's called the Rode Boom mic. It attaches right to the top of the video of the camera. That's another thing that I like about the Panasonic GH2. If you buy a camera or use a camera, you want to make sure that there's an external mic port, right? Because you don't want to use the built-in microphone on the camera because the audio will suck. And so you want to make sure that there's an external jack. And so this boom mic, which could have been, I don't even know, $60 or something like that, plugs right in and it allows me to record great audio without needing a lapel mic or something like that. Now I do sometimes use a lapel mic. I use a cheap Audio Technica ATR3350 that you can get for probably under $50 at Amazon. That plugs also into the camera. And I use that when I'm going to be quite a distance away from the camera. So I'm going to be quite a distance away from the boom mic. The boom mic you use when you are close to the camera, the lavalier mic, the lapel mic you use when you're going to be further away from the camera. Now for the iPhone, there's an awesome lapel mic, also like $50, $60, by RODE called the RODE Smart Lab. And if you go and you look for RODE Smart Lab for iPhone on Amazon, you'll be able to find it. You don't need to go out and buy all of these things. I just wanted to share with you, give you like I said, a peek behind our little toolbox. Now for graphics and images, this is one of the things that people asked me, where did you get the images for the four videos that you released as part of the video series leading up to the Six Figure Funnel Formula training program launch. So you can use iStock Photo. I didn't use iStock Photo for this launch, but I do often use iStock Photo for lots of different images. You always have to make sure when you're using images that they are royalty free. So you can't just go to someone's site and snag it and use it. You've got to make sure that they are royalty free. So we go right to iStock Photo. We use clipart.com and animationfactory.com. I think it was animationfactory.com where I got the bulk of the cool images for that video series. Any time I do a launch, and I haven't done one in a very long time, but when I used to do launches, let's say at strategic profits or in my other companies, I would just go and I would pay for like two weeks of unlimited at clipart.com. It could have been 40 bucks or something like that. And I would pay for like a membership at animation factory. It could have been another 50 bucks and then they let you download an unlimited number of images. And so just over the two weeks, I downloaded all the images that I wanted and used them. Now for video clips, like I released a video at the start of the pre-launch for the Six Figure Funnel Formula Training Program and I had like there were these Eagles flying. It was called the Lone Eagle Video. Well I use videoblocks.com. I think it's phenomenal. There is like a, you could pay like 99 bucks for the year and get unlimited downloads. They also have sounds, they have some audio files, some music, but they have full blown video clips that you can use in your marketing. For music, I primarily use audiojungle.com. For streaming the videos, I recommend either video, VimeoPro or Wistia.com. I use typically VimeoPro. That's the service that, not the free Vimeo, but VimeoPro, which I think is like 50 bucks for the year. I like VimeoPro because they will take a video and make sure that it's viewable on every mobile device, every smartphone and it's incredibly simple to use. Like I said, it's like 50 bucks for the year. For the launch of the Six Figure Funnel Formula Program, we used Wistia. The reason why we did that is because Wistia, even though we paid hundreds and hundreds of dollars in bandwidth just to stream our videos through Wistia for this launch because we had something like 27,000 video views. Wistia offers some cool features that you don't get at Vimeo if you want allowing people to pick up where they left off and a couple of other things. Those are the two services that you use. Wistia is a lot more expensive. Vimeo is the one that we primarily use when we're not killing the bandwidth. Now, let's talk a little bit about continuity programs. There are a variety of different continuity products, continuity programs, programs where people pay on an ongoing basis, either weekly, monthly, quarterly or annually. You've got a CD of the month, you've got a DVD of the month, you've got mastermind groups, coaching programs, online, offline newsletter, the most basic one, which is a membership site and of course you've got web-based software. And so, look, understand that. There are lots and lots of programs where you're delivering an audio every month or a video every month. I used to deliver a lesson where it was an on-camera lesson every month in front of the whiteboard via DVD. I took the audio from that video and I put it into a CD and I had the whole thing transcribed. So every month, and ironically, Speaker Fulfillment Services fulfilled that for us, every month they would get a packet that was a DVD, a CD and a transcript. I only recorded the video once and then I took the audio, had it transcribed, had the audio put on a CD and you could do the same thing online if you want. And so, these are the different continuity programs that you can have. There's also talking about membership sites for a second. There's a variety of different membership sites. There are membership sites where it is all user content. For example, one of my friends has a paid forum where it's basically a membership site, but all it is is a forum for higher-end marketers. The marketers pay to get access to that forum to be able to exchange ideas and so on. So don't only think about membership sites as a platform where you constantly need to create new content. The other thing is that with some of the tools that I'm going to show you in a second, you could have a membership site that has preloaded content and the content just drips out automatically for every new member. So you could have a membership site where it's preloaded with a year's worth of content and every week a new piece of content gets unlocked for every member. So every new member that comes in automatically gets access to the first content and then a week later they get access to the second piece of content. A week later, the third content all on autopilot without you having to do anything. That can also be done with a CD or DVD of the month. You can record a series of 12 DVDs or 12 CDs, for example, and just have it so that every new member starts at CD1 or CD2. At the end of 12 months, maybe the program is over. Maybe it's a time-based continuity program for 12 months. And so you can create all the DVDs or all the CDs or both and then every new member that comes in, they'll just be fulfilled automatically. You don't do anything and it's just ongoing for them. And so a couple of quick tools that we use, tools and resources. So for site development, when we're building all of our membership sites, we almost always build them with optimized press. It's one of the easiest page builders today that you can also secure content with Wishlist member. That's our digital delivery platform of choice. Wishlist member can be used so that only certain people will get access to certain content. So you can put your membership site behind Wishlist member. Wishlist member is basically a plugin that gets installed into the WordPress site that you've got optimized press on. Optimized press actually takes care of the look and feel. Wishlist member takes care of securing it so that only paid members get access to the content. And you can control memberships, levels, the whole nine drip content. It's phenomenal. It's better than what you actually need. Fulfilling physical products like newsletters, CD of the month, DVD of the month, that sort of thing. Like I said, speaker fulfillment services from Brett Ridgeway is the company that I recommend and have used. Now let's talk about branding, right? Product branding and imaging, the look and feel of your products. Well, one of the things that I recommend you do in terms of the covers is go to Amazon and look at the covers of the bestselling books in your particular niche. And you want to model your covers off of the look and feel off of those bestselling books in terms of their covers. Now some of the resources that you can use to do this, 99 designs, you can use Fiverr to outsource. And I'll give you a bunch of other outsourcing sites that you can go to to have these things designed. You can also use tools like there's GIMP, which is a free image editor, which is not of the same level as Photoshop, but it'll do everything that you need to, if you really wanted to learn it, to create some awesome covers. And in terms of creating your digital e-covers, if you've got a digital product and you want to have a physical representation of your product, there's no better source that you could go to in my opinion than myecovermaker.com. It's awesome. And it allows you to make covers for all different types of products, for software products, for books, for magazines, for reports, membership cards, videos, audios, CDs, CDs laying down, standing up with shadows. It allows you to create bundles and it's all pointing and click. And so, very cool little resource there. It could be like 10 bucks a month or something like that. Now let's talk about some advanced product creation tactics that I want you to start to think about. And some of these are killer, right? So the first one is creating volumes. And what I mean by that is that if you release a product, one of the best things that you can do is call it volume one, because what it does is it gives you the ability to come out with volume two and volume three, right? And so you come out, if you come out with a product for plumbers, that's the 10 different ways to generate referrals, call it volume one, because it gives you the ability to come out with volume two. And especially for the customers that really enjoy volume one, they will be much more likely to snatch up volume two when you come out with it. Another advanced product creation tactic is to come out with annual products, like the 2014 guide to plumber marketing tactics or whatever, right? Like the most effective marketing tactics for plumbers, the 2014 edition, because it gives you the ability to come out with the 2015 edition. This is why you see annual live events, right? Like the annual summit from GKIC or the annual traffic and conversion from Ryan and Perry. And you've got the ability to do that for all different types of products, right? And I want you to think because those become products that your former buyers will buy again and again. They're not only products that will sell, like if you do the research, they'll sell well year to year, but you'll get your prior customers to buy every year because it's got updated information. Another advanced tactic is to ride the back of a public launch. And so let's say in your particular niche, you see that somebody just launched a product and it was an expensive product. Let's say, I don't know, it was a $500 product or $1,000 product or $2,000 product on a particular topic. And it's within your scope, under your brand. It's a similar topic that you deal with. Well, you can come out with your version, a light version of that product, not stealing their product or knocking their product off, but just coming out with your own version and you're able to ride the back. You're able to ride off of all the marketing effort that that marketer did during the launch to create awareness about the tactic or process or system or method that was being taught. You're able to ride off of the back of all the work that they did to now potentially capture a percentage of the market that didn't have the money to pay the $1,000, $1,500 or $2,000, which is what the typical launch is priced at. And so riding off the back of a public launch. Another advanced tactic is starting with an affiliate product where somebody asked me the other day on a live training webinar where they asked, can you start with, what if you don't have an add-on, you don't have any other products to use for the reverse add-on or the mid-tier add-on or something like that? And I said, well, you can start by promoting an affiliate product and look to see if the affiliate product does well. If the affiliate product does well, well, that gives you insight into a product for you to create. So you start off by, if you identify that they want XYZ and you don't have a product for XYZ, you go out, you find an affiliate that's got a great product for XYZ, you put it in, if it converts well, then you go and you create your own product on XYZ. So you get 100% of the money instead of 50% of the money. There's also partnering where you get together with somebody else that has the knowledge and has the content and they provide the content, you provide the marketing and you split it and you could split it up to 50-50 or in some cases, you can split it as high as 80% to you, 20% to them. That's what Agora does, like my friend Michael Masterson, that's what they do with experts at Strategic Profits. That's what is done with experts, but that's up to you. I'll show you an example of that in just a second. Then there is creating value out of thin air. I'm just going to show you an example of that so that you understand what that is. You can provide a preloaded or branded thumb drive. That's cool, right? It's just a different unique way of giving content. Today you can purchase branded thumb drives for $3, $4, $5 a pop. And so you can preload those thumb drives with all of the content that you want to provide and then send it to the user. That's what Jay Abraham just recently did. He launched a tremendous offer for all of his prior material for $2,000 and it gets delivered to you on a preloaded thumb drive. It's not delivered online. He's not actually creating a physical product. It's all loaded on a thumb drive and he ships the thumb drive. Cost him $5 for the thumb drive. Let's call it another $5 for shipping, $10. He's selling it right now for $2,000. I'm going to skip that for right now for time purposes. This was a product that years ago I created. You could see actually you could see it's got this doctor's name over here. This doctor, we held a series of four tele-seminars that this doctor taught and I did the marketing and we sold this product for like $497. And so I partnered with him. He had the knowledge. I didn't have the knowledge that he had. This particular product was about the inner game, like the mental psychological side of business. Like I said, we sold this thing for $497 and we sold a whole bunch of them. This is the creating value out of thin air. This is going back to that first product that I created. What we did was another bonus was they got $525 worth of long distance practice building coaching and support. They got two critique coupons from me where they could send in any ad, any two ads and I would critique it. They were able to submit a marketing challenge certificate at any point. So if at any point they were having a marketing challenge, they were facing an obstacle, they didn't know what to do. They could fill this out, mail it in and they could claim a 30 minute tele-consultation. Now this is creating value out of thin air because there's nothing that needs to be created for this. Right? You offer this. It's something that was created for this, but it created value out of thin air. The other thing, let me be honest with you, the other thing is that there's what's called breakage with these type of components, meaning that a certain percentage of people will just never take advantage of these. So even though it adds value, it adds tremendous value to what is being offered. This was like I said, this was just one of the bonuses that they got and it adds value. It jacks up the value of the offer, large percentage of people will never take advantage of this. That's okay. That's their choice. As long as the people who do take advantage of this, you do fulfill on these critiques or these marketing challenges and these 30 minute consultations. I think something, it was like some insane number, like 97% never took advantage of this and it was like 80% never took advantage of either of these. It was their choice, but that's creating value out of thin air. Now, having products created for you where you can have a ghost writer, you can have a ghost writer who will write the product. Check this out. You can have somebody write the product, like actual write, create the book, create the script, whatever. Then you can have somebody else record it for you and then you can have somebody take that audio recording and make slides for it and turn it into a video. You've got one person who writes it, one person who records the audio, one person who turns the audio into video. Now you've got a product that's video, audio and transcript and you did nothing. These are the different sites that you can use. One of the things I will tell you is that this one down here, onlinejobs.ph, this is what you use when you're looking to hire somebody to come aboard on a regular basis for you. You only use this if you're looking to hire somebody, like bring somebody on your team. The others you can use for one-off jobs, one-off projects. The lowest quality of all these is Fiverr. When it comes to, I wouldn't go to Fiverr to have scripts created. I would go to Odesk or I would go to something like Elance or VWorker for scripts. Fiverr is lower and stuff. The key is that for written products, when you're having somebody write the product, and really I guess we could say certainly for audio, but especially for written products, go with native speakers of your language. If it is from the US and it's English, go with native English speakers or else you'll have massive spelling issues, grammatical issues, and you'll wind up with a ton of editing that has to be done. Those are resources that you can use to have the whole thing outsourced. You wouldn't believe what you can have outsourced. Like I'll tell you, I had this idea and I had the whole thing outsourced. I wanted to create a book. This was many years ago when I was working with Chiropractors, a book that basically doctors would get their name put on the cover, their name put on the spine, their name, picture, and bio on the back, and their contact information on the back as if they were the author of the book. What we did was I hired somebody to write the book. I hired somebody to edit the book. I hired somebody to lay out the book, and then I hired somebody to create a cover. I think back then we spent a total of $700 or so to have this created. And for years we sold this to doctors. Like we sold copies. They can get their own copy with, like I said, their name fully branded for them that they could give out, give away, sell, do whatever they wanted for like, I don't know, $600, $700 for a hundred or something like that. And so we only paid 700 bucks once to have it created, and then we were able to resell it over and over and over and over and over. So you wouldn't believe what you could have created with outsourcing done the right way. So now let's very quickly talk about, to wrap this up or to begin wrapping this up, the mindset of an A-player info marketer. So the first thing is, and you heard me talk about this before, is leverage. Leveraging everything that you do, looking for leverage points. And so what I mean by that is looking for different ways to repurpose content as many times as possible. Meaning, so don't just create a piece of content and use it in one location or use it for one product. How else can you repurpose that content? You could take blog posts, for example, turn them into videos and then create a video compilation product. You can take blog posts and create a compilation product just from those. You can create blog posts, have somebody do a voiceover for each of those, turn it into a CD, turn it into an audio series. You can sell the audios with the videos and the collection of blog posts. You can take videos, turn them into audios, take those audios, use them, turn those audios into transcripts. You can take transcripts of a product and take pieces of those transcripts and turn them into blog posts. Then you could take those transcripts and you could those blog posts and then you could create audios that you can then sell or you can use even as podcasts. You could take blog posts and again turn them into a compilation. There are so many different ways to leverage your existing content and repurpose content. And so the other thing that I want you to think about is going in the reverse order, meaning that let's say you've got a little product, your customer captivation offer, it's the first product that you sell. Let's say it's one video that you sell one video, one audio and a handout or something like that that goes with it. And you see that that customer captivation offer a selling like hotcakes for you. Well, you extend that product by building out more components, by taking that video and seeing what did you leave out, what didn't you cover, what could you cover more in depth in more detail. And then you could build out a bigger version of that. And so this is what you see being done from any time you see a front end product, a customer captivation product that sells really well, you see bigger and bigger versions of that product being built out on the back end. So somebody buys a video on XYZ from you for 26 bucks or 29 bucks. Well, then maybe you create a multi video training that sells for $199. Then maybe you have a full blown coaching program on it. Then maybe you have a full blown live event on it. Then maybe there's a variety of different ways. This is for example, what Bill Glazer did over at GKIC. He released a book on outrageous advertising. Actually, let me take a step back. He gave a presentation at a live event on outrageous advertising and got a great response and saw that there was a demand for that type of information. He released a book and the book sold like crazy. Behind the book, he had an outrageous advertising live event. From the live event, he had an outrageous advertising coaching program. See how he built it out bigger and bigger based on the response from the initial offer. The last thing that I'm going to cover is just you've got to be prolific. When you're an information marketer, you've got to be prolific in terms of content creation and in terms of repurposing content. You've always got to ask yourself what's next. For years, and I first heard Dan Kennedy say this eight, nine years ago, I used to have this framed on my desk, what's next? For example, you come out with a product and it's selling, well, what's next? What's the next product? What's the next offer that you can create to deliver more value to your customers to give more and to give them another opportunity to learn more, to grow more, to extract more value and to fulfill their desire to invest, to get more. What's next? You need to be prolific with your information content. Now, to just wrap this up, like I mentioned earlier, this training was really all about showing you that there are so many different ways to create products. But at the end of the day, it doesn't matter what the product is, unless you have a marketing funnel that converts, that's able to take cold traffic, traffic, and convert that traffic into customers, into buyers. It doesn't matter how great your product is. It doesn't matter how many products you have, you have to have a marketing funnel to sell your products. It's the marketing funnel where the money is made. It's the marketing funnel that has the highest value.
